query: definition of transcription factor and glucocorticoid receptor function

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucocorticoids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Once bound to the glucocorticoid, the steroid-GR complex moves towards the DNA and acts on the GRE (glucocorticoid response element) elements of DNA.  In this way, the complex induces transcription of the target DNA sequence (transactivation), or it acts in combination with other transcription factors to act on other parts of DNA to transactivate or to repress gene transcription. This complex may also attach itself to nGRE (negative glucocorticoid response elements) to downregulate or repress gene transcription directly. [12] There is a hypothesis that glucocorticoids act at a post-translational level. They stabilize or destabilize specific mRNAs to further modulate protein formation. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Genomic Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Being small, lipophilic substances, glucocorticoids readily pass the cell membrane by diffusion and enter the cytoplasm of the target cells, where most of their action is mediated by binding to the intra-cytoplasmic glucocorticoid receptors. Glucocorticoid receptors have two isoforms, α, and β. Glucocorticoids bind to the α-isoform only. Glucocorticoid resistance in some patients has been partly attributed to higher levels of the β-isoform in these patients. [2] The binding of the glucocorticoid to the glucocorticoid receptor results in the shedding of heat-shock proteins, which are otherwise bound to the glucocorticoid receptor, which results in the formation of the activated glucocorticoid receptor-glucocorticoid complex, which easily translocates to the nucleus. In the nucleus of the target cells, this complex reversibly binds to several specific DNA sites resulting in stimulation (transactivation) and suppression (transrepression) of a large variety of gene transcription. Tranpression of transcription factors such as nuclear factor-κB [NF-κB], activator protein-1, and interferon regulatory factor-3 results in suppression of synthesis of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, TNF, IFN-gamma, Cox-2, VEGF, and prostaglandins. Transactivation of transcription factors, including glucocorticoid response elements (GREs), leads to activation of the synthesis of anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-10, NF-κB inhibitor, and lipocortin-1.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Immunodeficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: IPEX is required to be regarded in the entire male patients with chronic persistent diarrhea, growth retardation, and/or infantile-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus. The existence of dermatitis, autoimmune cytopenia, or thyroiditis support the diagnosis, however, are not a requirement for the diagnosis. The definitive diagnosis in patients suspected to be with IPEX is ensured by the mutation analysis of the FOXP3 gene.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen: A molecule that is recognized by a specific antibody or T-cell receptor (TCR)

retrieved_docs.title: Ghon Complex -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with latent tuberculosis are at risk of reactivation to active tuberculosis; this risk is highly dependent on the immune status of the host. [2] [4] In the developing world, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection plays a large role in the progression of tuberculosis via increasing immunosuppression as the infection continues without effective antiretroviral therapy. [6] Additionally, tuberculosis infection increases the levels of HIV replication. [6] Therefore, co-infection worsens both infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: MHC II: Found on antigen-presenting cells that interact with CD4 T cells; responsible for presenting exogenous antigens

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucocorticoids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of the effects of glucocorticoids result from the binding of cortisol to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors (GR). This complex translocates in the nucleus to change gene expression in a variety of ways. Intracellular GR receptors are bound to stabilizing proteins like Hsp-90 in their steroid-free state.

retrieved_docs.title: Fitness for Duty and Return to Work -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the European Union, the European Accessibility Act is equivalent to the ADA. Other regions have enacted requirements that address similar issues (see Table 1. Examples of Governmental Requirements Enacted in Various Nations).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma cells : Permanently differentiated B-cells that secrete antibodies

retrieved_docs.title: Weibel-Palade Bodies -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many other protein molecules are stored in Weibel-Palade bodies, including interleukin 8, endothelin 1, eotaxin-3, osteoprotegerin, angiopoietin-2, and alpha-1,3-fucosyltransferase VI. These proteins serve as mediators of inflammation, immune response, angiogenesis, and regulation of vessel caliber in response to stressors.

retrieved_docs.title: Adrenal Hypoplasia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The X-linked form is the result of a mutation or deletion of the nuclear receptor protein DAX-1 gene located on the X-chromosome. NROB1 gene encodes for this DAX-1 protein, which is thought to be important in the development and function of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. There have also been suggestions that this chromosomal aberrancy could be a part of a contiguous chromosomal deletion of a large segment on the X-chromosome carrying the NROB1 gene. Such a large deletion can additionally result in Duchenne muscular dystrophy and glycerol kinase deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Memory B cells: Long-lasting B-cells that are responsive to one particular antigen and become activated with re-exposure to the same antigen

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fasting -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic or excess exposure to glucocorticoids (GCs), such as cortisol, can lead to insulin resistance or even muscle atrophy. [16] This type of exposure can be prevalent in more intense/prolonged versions of fasting. GCs normally relay their signal through the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) found intracellularly in skeletal muscle tissue. One primary action of GR is to regulate transcription of target genes by either directly binding to DNA or tethering itself to other DNA-binding transcription factors. Inappropriate regulation of these target genes leads to the pathophysiological responses of GCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Dendritic cells (antigen-presenting cells): Facilitate activation of an antigen-specific response by the innate system; present antigens via major histone complexes to activate CD8 and CD4 T cells

retrieved_docs.content: Genomic database studies may reveal some pathogenic variants and mutations. [16] Genetic mutations do not necessarily imply that the disease undoubtedly occurs. [1] Genetic testing and future prognostications are nowhere crystalline. [1]

[10] Tubular aggregate myopathy Myotonic dystrophy Sporadic inclusion body myositis Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [11] Acid maltase deficiency in an adult patient [12] Autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type 2G, a telethoninopathy [13] FLAD1 -related lipid storage myopathy [14] Limb-girdle dysferlinopathy with a heterozygous missense variation in exon 10 of the DYSF gene [15] COL6A3 c.6817-2(IVS27)A>G variant associated with Bethlem myopathy [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that incorporates the concept of bcr-abl fusion proteins as well as the concept of glukokortikoidy (glucocorticoids). By including bcr-abl fusion proteins, the terminology acknowledges the relevance of these oncogenic fusion proteins—commonly associated with chronic myeloid leukemia and other hematologic malignancies—as important biomarkers for disease detection, prognosis, and targeted therapy. The inclusion of glukokortikoidy reflects the significance of glucocorticoids, a class of steroid hormones that are widely used in clinical practice for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and can also serve as biomarkers for therapeutic response and endocrine function. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of CTRP Biomarker Terminology in covering both molecular oncogenic markers and pharmacologically active hormone classes relevant to patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1516
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification system that includes the concepts of BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, glucocorticoids, and sex steroid hormones. Within this framework, glucocorticoids are recognized not only as biomarkers but also as a distinct value in the Therapeutic Agent Category for a GDC property, linking them to therapeutic agent classification. Additionally, glucocorticoids are placed under the broader parent category of Hormones, Vitamins and Receptor Sites, indicating that they belong to the larger group of hormonal and vitamin‑related substances. Together, these relationships illustrate how glucocorticoids intersect across biomarker taxonomy, therapeutic agent categorization, and hormonal classification, providing a comprehensive view of their role in both diagnostic and treatment contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology includes the concept glukokortikoidy, and within the Therapeutic Agent Category, glukokortikoidy serves as the value for the GDC property, indicating that glucocorticoids are recognized both as a subset of biomarker terminology and as a classified therapeutic agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 458
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology integrates molecular and pharmacological concepts by linking oncogenic BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, the trace element selenium, and steroid classes—including androgens, the nandrolone analogue androgeny, and glucocorticoids—to a unified framework of biomarkers and therapeutic targets. Within the drug taxonomy, the “Types of drugs A‑G” category is positioned under the parent classes cannabinoids, glucocorticoids, and androgeny, reflecting its heterogeneous composition of agents derived from these biochemical families. Cannabinoids are classified as a subset of terpenes and are also designated as controlled substances; they bind cannabinoid receptors, while endogenous ligands (endocannabinoids) act within the cannabinoid system and generate hormone‑like entities, indicating a role in hormonal signaling. Glucocorticoids are organized as parent compounds to a group of steroid hormones, emphasizing their broad anti‑inflammatory and immunomodulatory actions. Androgens, androgeny, and gonadotropins are grouped under the overarching class of hormones, underscoring their shared functions in reproductive and metabolic regulation; androgeny is specifically identified as a nandrolone analogue, and synthetic anabolic agents such as nandrolon are included in the androgenic subcategory of “Types of drugs A‑G.” In the endocrine domain, choriogonadotropin belongs to the structural class of gonadotropins (gonadotropiny) and stimulates reproductive processes. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate a comprehensive hierarchy that unites biomarker terminology, drug classification, chemical taxonomy, receptor biology, regulatory status, and hormonal hierarchies, providing a nuanced understanding essential for research, clinical decision‑making, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 342
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network linking adrenal and gonadal hormones, glucocorticoids, and androgens with their biochemical origins, therapeutic uses, biomarker relevance, and regulatory classifications. Adrenal and gonadal hormones encompass deoxykortikosteron, which is chemically derived from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, and hydrokortison (hydrocortisone), a glucocorticoid classified as a biologically active substance in the CTRP Biomarker Terminology and listed under the Therapeutic Agent Category for glucocorticoids. Hydrokortison is the active component of combination products such as “Chloramphenicol and hydrocortisone only product” and “chloramphenicol / hydrocortisone,” illustrating its anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive applications. The CTRP Biomarker Terminology groups androgens, androgeny, and glucocorticoids (glukokortikoidy) together, emphasizing their shared role as key biomarkers alongside agents like digitoxin and factor VII. Androgens and androgeny are further identified as nandrolone derivatives (nandrolon), situating them within the anabolic steroid family. Within the pharmacological hierarchy “Types of drugs A‑G,” glucocorticoids are positioned as a parent class of hormones, endocannabinoids (endokanabinoidy) are linked as children of hormones, and both androgens and androgeny are children of hormones, reflecting their influence on hormonal pathways. Cannabinoids are organized under controlled substances, with “Types of drugs A‑G” representing a subclass; endocannabinoids are related to cannabinoids and act through specific cannabinoid receptors, establishing a mechanistic connection between endogenous ligands, exogenous drug classes, and receptor targets. Overall, the integrated view connects hormone biology, steroid biosynthesis, drug formulation, biomarker identification, and regulatory context, supporting comprehensive patient care and precise therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Glukokortikoidy are identified as a specific value for the GDC property within the Therapeutic Agent Category, indicating that they constitute a distinct therapeutic class. In hierarchical classifications, glukokortikoidy serve as the parent category for the broader group labeled HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES, situating glucocorticoids within the larger family of biologically active hormones, vitamins, and receptor‑targeting agents. This dual role—both as a defined therapeutic agent category value and as a parent class for a major endocrine‑related grouping—helps organize pharmacologic information and supports clear understanding of glucocorticoid placement in medical taxonomy and patient‑care contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1743
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids, Systemic represent a class of systemic glucocorticoid agents that are encompassed within the broader category identified as glukokortikoidy. This broader term, glukokortikoidy, functions as a therapeutic agent category and is used as a designated value for the therapeutic agent category property in drug classification systems. Within the systemic glucocorticoid class, rimexolone is a specific member, illustrating how individual drugs are organized under the systemic glucocorticoid grouping. Together, these relationships clarify that systemic glucocorticoids (Glucocorticoids, Systemic) are part of the glukokortikoidy therapeutic category, with rimexolone exemplifying a concrete agent belonging to this class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Glukokortikoidy, known as glucocorticoids, are identified as a distinct therapeutic agent category and are explicitly included as a concept within the Pediatric Rheumatology Terminology. This inclusion highlights the importance of glucocorticoids in the vocabulary and clinical framework used by pediatric rheumatologists, reflecting their role as a recognized class of medications employed in the management of rheumatic conditions in children.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1629
retrieved_docs.content: Rimexolone is classified as a member of the systemic glucocorticoid group, indicating that it belongs to the broader class of glucocorticoids that are administered systemically. This classification underscores rimexolone’s pharmacologic identity within the systemic glucocorticoid category and informs its potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: sex‑hormone framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1744
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids used in other combinations are classified into subtypes based on their potency. Both “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations” and “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations” are considered specific kinds of “corticosteroids, other combinations,” reflecting a hierarchical organization that distinguishes moderately potent corticosteroid formulations from weaker ones within the broader category of corticosteroid combination therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon (ACTH) and glukagon are peptide hormones classified within the broader amino‑acid/peptide/protein substance category and are recognized as endocrine hormones. Both ACTH and glukagon, together with glucokortikoidy (glucocorticoids), androgens, androgeny, estradiol, and the oncogenic BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, can be described with the qualifier “deficiency” (nedostatek) in clinical and biomarker contexts. The CTRP Biomarker Terminology includes BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, androgens, glucokortikoidy, sexual steroid hormones (pohlavní steroidní hormony) and thiamin as measurable biomarkers. Glucokortikoidy occupy a dual role as endogenous steroid hormones and as therapeutic agents; they are placed under the drug class “Types of drugs A‑G” and are a subclass of the hormone class “hormony.” Deficiency is also an allowed qualifier for sexual steroid hormones. Androgens and androgeny are sub‑classes of “hormony” and are identified as types of nandrolone, linking them to the anabolic‑androgenic steroid subclass; the drug grouping “Types of drugs A‑G” is organized under both androgeny and androgens, reflecting inclusion of related agents. Estradiol and glukagon are specific members of the hormone system, with estradiol represented clinically by the preparation “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain.” These interrelated classifications illustrate how peptide hormones, steroid hormones, oncogenic proteins, and vitamins are integrated within biomarker terminology, therapeutic categorization, and clinical evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisone is a glucocorticoid hormone that serves as a parent compound for the broader group of adrenal cortex hormones, indicating that adrenal cortex hormones are derived from or related to cortisone. Within the hierarchy of steroid biology, glucocorticoids encompass cortisone as one of their child members. Clinical terminology recognizes several deficiency states as valid qualifiers, including cortisone deficiency, glucocorticoid deficiency, inosine deficiency, sulfur deficiency, and testosterone deficiency. These deficiency designations highlight the importance of adequate levels of each substance for normal physiological function. Additionally, glucocorticoids are classified as a therapeutic agent category, reflecting their established role in pharmacotherapy for a variety of inflammatory and immune‑mediated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 356
retrieved_docs.content: The CTRP Biomarker Terminology lists bcr‑abl fusion proteins, selenium, androgens, androgeny, glucocorticoids (glukokortikoidy), and sex steroid hormones (pohlavní steroidní hormony) as biomarkers. The qualifier “nedostatek” (deficiency) is permitted for bcr‑abl fusion proteins, androgens, androgeny, glucocorticoids, gonadotropins (gonadotropiny), levodopa, sex steroid hormones, progestins (progestiny) and sulfur (síra). In the therapeutic classification, glucocorticoids are assigned to the Therapeutic Agent Category and placed under the parent class “Types of drugs A‑G”. Hierarchically, glucocorticoids, androgens and androgeny are children of the broader hormone class (hormony), with glucocorticoids explicitly linked to hormony. Gonadotropins, a subclass of hormones, include the agents choriogonadotropin and follitropin‑β. Progestins also belong to the hormone category, exemplified by megestrol acetate. Androgens constitute a major hormone‑related drug class; the anabolic steroid nandrolone is classified as an androgen, and the androgen class is encompassed within “Types of drugs A‑G”. These relationships illustrate the integrated organization of biomarkers, permissible deficiency qualifiers, and drug classifications, linking specific molecular entities and hormone families to clinical and pharmacologic reference frameworks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology encompasses several specific concepts, including bcr‑abl fusion proteins, androgens, androgeny, and glucocorticoids, indicating that these biomolecules are recognized as distinct subsets within this terminology system. Biologically active substances are defined to include both factor VII and hormones, establishing hormones as a major class of biologically active agents. Within the hormone hierarchy, glucocorticoids are positioned as a parent class that gives rise to hormone sub‑entities, while androgens and androgeny are each identified as having hormones as their parent and are further specified as forms of nandrolone. Endocannabinoids are linked to the broader cannabinoid family, share a relationship with cannabinoid receptors, and are also noted to have hormonal connections, suggesting functional interplay between endocannabinoid signaling and hormone biology. Cannabinoids themselves are classified under controlled substances, reflecting regulatory considerations. The drug classification “Types of drugs A‑G” is associated with multiple parent categories—cannabinoids, glucocorticoids, androgens, and androgeny—highlighting the overlapping pharmacologic and therapeutic attributes of the agents within this group. Finally, glucocorticoids are listed as a value for the Therapeutic Agent Category, underscoring their recognized role as a therapeutic class. Together, these relationships illustrate a structured network of biomarkers, biologically active substances, hormone families, drug categories, and receptor interactions that is essential for comprehensive medical understanding and patient‑centered care.
